lesson:
氏 (family name/clan) + 名 (given name) = full name. But you'll almost never hear this word in conversation. 氏名 lives on forms, documents, official applications, and bureaucratic instructions. It's the "full legal name" version of asking who you are.

If you fill out any paperwork in Japan — at a hotel, a bank, a government office, a doctor — you'll see 氏名 printed next to a blank line. That's where your full name goes. Sometimes the form separates it into 氏 (family name) and 名 (given name) on separate lines. In Japan, family name comes first — always.

In casual life, you'd use 名前 (なまえ) for "name." 氏名 is strictly for forms and official contexts. Knowing this word is less about conversation and more about literacy — recognizing it when you see it on paper.

今 (now/this) + 夜 (night) = tonight. The 今 compound machine keeps working: 今日 (today), 今週 (this week), 今月 (this month), 今年 (this year), and now 今夜 (tonight). Same pattern every time.

The reading こんや uses Chinese-derived こん for 今 (like 今週, 今月) and や for 夜. Compare with 今晩 (こんばん, this evening) — which you already know from こんばんは (good evening). The two are nearly interchangeable, but 今夜 leans slightly more toward late night while 今晩 covers the broader evening.

今夜は何をする？ (what are you doing tonight?) — a simple, high-frequency question.

夜 (night) + 中 (middle) = the middle of the night. Not midnight on the dot (that's 零時 or 十二時), but the general dead-of-night zone — roughly midnight to 3 or 4 AM, when the world is asleep. 夜中に目が覚めた (I woke up in the middle of the night).

夜中 carries a mood. It implies darkness, quiet, a time when you're not supposed to be awake. 夜中にうるさい (loud in the middle of the night) is a complaint. 夜中まで勉強した (studied until the middle of the night) implies exhausting effort. Things that happen 夜中に are either desperate, illicit, or the mark of serious dedication.

The 中 here works the same way as in 昼間 (ひるま, daytime — the middle of the day-period). It pins you to the core of a time span, not its edges.

来 (come/next) + 週 (week) = the coming week. The 来 prefix is the future counterpart to 先 (last) and 今 (this): 先週 → 今週 → 来週. Last week, this week, next week.

The same 来 pattern scales up: 来月 (らいげつ, next month), 来年 (らいねん, next year). And going backward, 先 handles the past: 先週, 先月. This three-part time system is completely regular — one of the few things in Japanese you can just slot in mechanically.

来週の月曜日 (next Monday). 来週までに (by next week). 来週はどう？ (how about next week?). No particles needed after 来週 when it's a time reference — it works as a bare time noun, just like 今日 or 明日.

You already know the rule from いつ: question word + か = indefinite. いつか = someday. どこか = somewhere. And now: 誰か = someone. The か particle strips the question-ness away and replaces it with vagueness — you're no longer asking "who?" but referring to an unspecified person.

誰かいる？ (is someone there?). 誰かに聞いて (ask someone). 誰か来た (someone came). Notice that particles like が are often dropped after 誰か in casual speech — 誰かが来た and 誰か来た both work, but the second is more natural in conversation.

Keep building your question-word transformation table: 誰か (someone), 誰も + negative (no one), 誰でも (anyone). Same pattern as every other question word. Once you have it for one, you have it for all of them.

You already know 何 read as なに — "what." This entry is for the other reading: なん. Same kanji, same meaning, but used in different phonetic contexts. The rule is roughly: use なん before で, の, and だ/です, and before counters. Use なに everywhere else.

何ですか (なんですか, what is it?) — なん because です follows. 何で (なんで, why? / by what means?) — なん because で follows. 何人 (なんにん, how many people?) — なん before a counter. But: 何を食べる？ (なにをたべる, what will you eat?) — なに because を follows.

The distinction is phonetic convenience — なに before で would create a clumsy なにで, while なんで flows better. Your ear will pick this up quickly; it's one of those things that sounds wrong before you can explain why.

今朝 is one of those words where the reading has nothing to do with the individual kanji readings. 今 is normally いま or こん. 朝 is normally あさ or ちょう. But together? けさ. No trace of either reading. This is a 熟字訓 (jukujikun) — a compound where the meaning is right (now + morning = this morning) but the reading is a separate native Japanese word plastered on top of the kanji. けさ is an ancient Japanese word for "this morning" that predates the kanji assignment.

You've already seen this phenomenon: 今日 (きょう), 今年 (ことし), 昨日 (きのう), 明日 (あした). Time words are especially prone to irregular readings because the Japanese time vocabulary is old — older than the kanji system they borrowed from Chinese. The native words refused to be replaced.

今朝は寒かった (it was cold this morning). No particles needed — bare time noun, like all the others.

The kanji 寿司 are 当て字 (ateji) — characters chosen for their sounds, not their meanings. 寿 means "longevity" and 司 means "to manage/govern." Neither has anything to do with vinegared rice. They were picked because they sound like すし and happen to look auspicious. You'll also see sushi written as 鮨 (fish + delicious) or 鮓, which are older and more etymologically honest. But 寿司 won the popularity contest because those "longevity" associations made it perfect for celebrations.

The word すし originally referred to the preservation method — fish pressed into fermented rice, left for months. The quick, fresh sushi you know (握り寿司, にぎりずし, hand-pressed sushi) is a relatively recent Tokyo invention from the early 1800s. Street food that went upscale.

寿司を食べに行こう (let's go eat sushi). Note the construction: 〜に行く (go in order to do something). This is the sentence that starts many good evenings.

meaning: to wear (below the waist), put on (below the waist)
lesson:
Japanese splits "to wear" into different verbs based on what body part the clothing goes on. 着る (きる) is for upper body — shirts, jackets, dresses. 被る (かぶる) is for the head — hats, helmets. And 履く is for everything below the waist: pants, skirts, shoes, socks. ズボンを履く (put on pants). 靴を履く (put on shoes). 靴下を履く (put on socks).

The dividing line is roughly the waist. If it goes on your legs or feet, 履く. This verb doesn't exist in English — we just "put on" everything regardless — so it takes conscious practice. The mental model: 履く involves stepping into something or sliding it up your legs.

The kanji 履 contains 尸 (body) and 復 (repeat/return), suggesting repeated steps — feet in motion, feet in footwear. It's the same kanji in 履歴書 (りれきしょ, resume — literally a "record of footsteps").

Particles: 履く takes を — the garment or footwear is your direct object. 靴を履く, ジーンズを履く.

Potential が履ける: が — what you can wear becomes the subject. このサイズの靴が履ける (I can wear shoes this size).

Passive に履かれる: rare — clothing isn't usually the agent. But: スリッパを履かれてしまった (someone wore my slippers — and now I'm annoyed).

Causative に…を履かせる: に marks who you dress, を marks what. 子供に靴を履かせる (put shoes on the kid).

五段 Godan他動詞
Causativeはかせるに／を
Caus-passはかせられるに
Potentialはけるが
Imperativeはけを
Imp なさいはきなさいを
Cond ～ばはけばを
Cond ～たらはいたらを

おじさん means uncle — or more broadly, any middle-aged man. Just as English might use "mister" loosely, Japanese uses おじさん for any man who looks roughly 40-60. The kanji, when written, depends on the relationship: 伯父さん for your parent's older brother, 叔父さん for your parent's younger brother. In speech, both are just おじさん.

The length trap again: おじさん (uncle, short じ) vs. おじいさん (grandfather, long じい). One extra mora is the difference between middle-aged and elderly. This is the same pair from the おじいさん lesson, now seen from the other side. Get the vowel length wrong and you're accidentally calling someone ancient — or weirdly young.

When talking about your own uncle to someone outside your family, the humble form is おじ — drop the さん. Same in-group/out-group rule as all Japanese family terms.

The female counterpart to おじさん. おばさん means aunt or any middle-aged woman. The kanji split: 伯母さん for your parent's older sister, 叔母さん for your parent's younger sister. Spoken, both are おばさん.

And the same vowel-length minefield applies: おばさん (aunt/middle-aged woman, short ば) vs. おばあさん (grandmother/elderly woman, long ばあ). Getting this wrong is socially risky in exactly the same way as the おじ/おじい pair. Japanese people are acutely aware of this distinction, and calling a woman おばあさん when she considers herself おばさん will not go over well.

In contemporary Japanese, some women have started to reclaim or joke about the おばさん label — おばさん力 (おばさんりょく, "auntie power") refers to the boldness and social fearlessness that stereotypically comes with middle age. The word is getting a cultural upgrade.

The grandmother counterpart to おじいさん. おばあさん is someone else's grandmother, or the word you use addressing your own grandmother directly. When referring to your own grandmother in conversation with outsiders, use the humble 祖母 (そぼ).

The word also applies to any elderly woman — おばあさん at the neighborhood shop, おばあさん in a fairy tale. Japanese folklore is full of おじいさん and おばあさん pairs — 昔々、おじいさんとおばあさんがいました (once upon a time, there lived an old man and an old woman) is the standard opening for traditional stories, the Japanese equivalent of "once upon a time."

The four-level system is now complete: おばさん (aunt/middle-aged) → おばあさん (grandmother/elderly). おじさん (uncle/middle-aged) → おじいさん (grandfather/elderly). Short vowel = younger generation. Long vowel = older generation. Four words, one vowel-length rule.

いとこ is one of those words that can be written with an absurd number of kanji combinations — 従兄弟, 従姉妹, 従兄, 従妹, and more, depending on whether the cousin is male or female, older or younger. In practice, almost everyone just writes it in hiragana: いとこ. The kanji options exist but they're a headache nobody wants.

Japanese cousin terminology reflects a culture that cares about relative age. Technically, an older male cousin is 従兄 (じゅうけい), a younger female cousin is 従妹 (じゅうまい), and so on. But spoken Japanese just uses いとこ for everyone, regardless of gender or age — the simplification happened naturally because the formal terms were too unwieldy for conversation.

The 従 in the kanji means "follow/accompany" — a cousin follows alongside your direct family line without being part of it. Etymologically neat, practically ignored.

辞 (word/expression) + 書 (book/writing) = word-book. A dictionary. The kanji 辞 is specifically about language and vocabulary — you'll see it in 辞典 (じてん, dictionary — slightly more formal) and 辞める (やめる, to resign — originally "to decline with words").

辞書 vs 辞典 vs 字典: All mean "dictionary" but with different scopes. 辞書 is the general everyday word. 辞典 sounds more formal, more comprehensive — an encyclopedia-style reference. 字典 (じてん, same pronunciation) is specifically a kanji dictionary. In daily speech, 辞書 covers everything. 辞書で調べる (look it up in the dictionary) — the sentence every language learner lives by.

Electronic dictionaries (電子辞書) were once ubiquitous in Japan — dedicated handheld devices that every student carried. Smartphones have largely replaced them, but you'll still see the term and occasionally the devices in schools.

朝 (morning) + ご飯 (cooked rice / meal) = morning meal. Breakfast. The pattern is dead simple and produces all three meals: 朝ご飯 (breakfast), 昼ご飯 (ひるごはん, lunch), 晩ご飯 (ばんごはん, dinner). Morning rice, noon rice, evening rice. Three words for the price of one pattern.

The ご in ご飯 is an honorific prefix — even rice gets polite treatment. Dropping it (朝飯, あさめし) sounds rougher, more masculine, more casual. Same meal, different social register.

A traditional Japanese 朝ご飯 looks nothing like a Western breakfast: rice, miso soup, grilled fish, pickled vegetables, a raw egg, natto (fermented soybeans). No pancakes, no cereal. Knowing 朝ご飯 without knowing what it typically contains is missing half the picture.

You already know 白い (しろい, the adjective "white"). This entry is 白 the noun — the color white as a thing. 白が好き (I like white). 白にする (I'll go with white). When you're choosing a color — for a car, a phone case, a t-shirt — you name the noun form.

Japanese color words have this adjective/noun split for the traditional colors: 白い/白, 黒い/黒, 赤い/赤, 青い/青. The adjective describes something (白い猫, a white cat). The noun names the color itself (白, white).

In Japanese culture, 白 is the color of purity, death, and new beginnings simultaneously. Wedding kimonos are white. Funeral offerings use white. Blank paper is a white beginning. The color carries a weight that the English "white" doesn't quite match — it's closer to "blank" or "clean slate" than to any single symbolism.

どっち is the casual contraction of どちら — "which one" when choosing between two things. どっちがいい？ (which one do you want?). どっちでもいい (either is fine). The っ gives it a snappy, informal punch that どちら doesn't have.

The full set: こっち (this way/mine), そっち (that way/yours), あっち (over there), どっち (which way?). These are the ko-so-a-do directional pronouns in casual form. Use them with friends, family, anyone you're comfortable with. Switch to こちら/そちら/あちら/どちら for politeness.

どっち only works for two options. For three or more, you need どれ. This two-vs-many distinction doesn't exist in casual English ("which one" works for any number), but Japanese insists on it. Two choices: どっち. Three or more: どれ.

The casual version of そちら — "that way" or "your side." Where こっち is my zone, そっち is your zone. そっちはどう？ (how is it over there? / how about on your end?). そっちに行く (I'll go to where you are).

On the phone, そっち is especially useful. When you can't see the other person, space becomes abstract — そっち means "your situation, your side of things." そっちの天気はどう？ (how's the weather there?). そっちは忙しい？ (are you busy on your end?).

Remember the register split: そっち for casual, そちら for polite. Using そっち with your boss would be like saying "your end" instead of "if I may ask about your situation" — technically the same meaning, but the casualness stands out.

明 (bright) + 日 (day) = bright day, tomorrow. The kanji make poetic sense — tomorrow is the day that dawns bright and new. But the reading あした has nothing to do with either 明 (normally めい/あかるい) or 日 (normally にち/ひ). It's another 熟字訓 — a native Japanese time word that got kanji assigned for meaning, not sound.

明日 actually has three readings: あした (casual, most common), あす (slightly more formal, used in news and writing), and みょうにち (formal/official, used in announcements). Same kanji, three registers. In daily life, あした is what you use.

明日何する？ (what are you doing tomorrow?). また明日 (see you tomorrow) — one of the most common partings in Japanese. Two words, no verb needed.

明後日: 明 (bright/next) + 後 (after) + 日 (day). The day after the next day. And the reading あさって is, once again, a native word that ignores the individual kanji sounds.

English doesn't have a single common word for this concept — "the day after tomorrow" is a four-word phrase. Japanese gives it one word, あさって, spoken in four syllables. The fact that it exists as a single word tells you something about how Japanese organizes time: the near future gets named precisely. 明日 (tomorrow), 明後日 (day after tomorrow), and even 明々後日 (しあさって, three days from now). Each gets its own word. Beyond that, you switch to counting: 四日後 (four days later).

明後日は休みだ (the day after tomorrow is a day off). A handy word for planning anything — no awkward arithmetic required.

The mirror image of 明後日, looking backward instead of forward. 一昨日: 一 (one) + 昨 (previous) + 日 (day). And the reading おととい — five syllables of native Japanese that couldn't care less what the kanji say.

The complete near-time lineup now looks like this, centered on today:

一昨日 (おととい, day before yesterday)
昨日 (きのう, yesterday)
今日 (きょう, today)
明日 (あした, tomorrow)
明後日 (あさって, day after tomorrow)

Five days, five words, all with irregular readings. If this seems like a lot of memorization, it is — but they're such high-frequency words that they'll stick fast through sheer repetition. You'll hear おととい whenever someone is talking about recent events: おととい買ったばかりなのに (I just bought it the day before yesterday, and yet...) — a common setup for a complaint about something breaking too soon.
